fr:mainmatter.html:p: "Ram is not always ram". Referring to the fact that a physical memory access takes a substantial different amount of time depending on whether it hit in the TLB.
A small hardware cache of recent virtual-to-physical translations. Without it, every memory reference would incur the cost of walking the Page table in memory.

html:ol.html:li: Extract VPN from the virtual address.
Check whether the VPN is in the TLB.
TLB hit: read PPN from the entry, append offset, access memory.
TLB miss: walk the page table (in hardware on CISC, by trapping to OS on RISC), insert the translation into the TLB, retry.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A TLB entry holds the VPN, the PPN, a valid bit (separate from the PTE valid bit — invalid on init, eviction, context switch), and protection bits.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. Each process has different translations for the same VPN, so a naive TLB must be flushed on every context switch.
Solution. Add an address space identifier (ASID) to each TLB entry and an ASID register set on context switch. Now entries from different processes coexist.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: "Ram is not always ram". Referring to the fact that a physical memory access takes a substantial different amount of time depending on whether it hit in the TLB.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. In real software-managed TLBs, an entry has global bits for shared pages, an 8-bit ASID, coherence bits, and a wired register reserving slots for code that cannot afford a TLB miss (e.g. the TLB-miss handler itself).
Remark. For very fast caches, even a TLB access is a bottleneck. Virtually indexed caches sidestep the TLB on the index path.
